All reports
REDX

Voice of Customer Report

What real estate agents say about prospecting tools in their own words, and what to do with it.

Prepared by [Author - to be filled]
June 2026

0. Executive Summary

Bottom line. Agents who succeed with REDX describe it the same way every time: a system that pays off when they do the work. The single loudest complaint, across REDX and every rival alike, is data accuracy: bad, disconnected, and DNC-flagged numbers, and “expired” leads that sold years ago. This report reads entirely from public reviews and complaints, with no first-party customer interviews this engagement, so the first recommended next step is a primary-interview pass to confirm and sharpen what the public record shows.

Three things customers say they want, in their own words.

First, a tool they can build a business on if they put in the effort. “What I like most about RedX is that it is a tool that you can build your business with just that one tool alone (if you are willing to do the work).”1 The parenthetical is the whole story: the value is conditional, and the best customers say so themselves.

Second, leads that connect to a real owner who can still transact. “Too many ‘bad’ phone numbers. Hard to know which ones are ‘good’.”2 This is the want underneath nearly every negative review, REDX and competitor.

Third, a system that keeps them disciplined. “REDX keeps me disciplined in lead management, providing and organizing leads effectively for follow-up.”3 Agents do not buy a list. They buy a reason to dial every morning.

The biggest gap between how REDX talks and how customers talk sits on the word data. Vendor language across this category leans on accurate and verified and motivated sellers. Customers describe “bad numbers,” numbers “on the DNC,” and owners whose “houses sold years ago.” Accuracy complaints land on Vulcan7 and Landvoice too,17 so this is a category table-stakes problem, not a REDX-only failing. The single highest-value language shift is to stop selling on data purity and start selling on the conditional promise the happy customers already voice: a complete system plus the coaching that makes a working agent’s effort convert.

0.5 Evidence Base and Recommended Primary Research

This report is built entirely from public sources: customer reviews on G2, Trustindex/Google, Software Finder, and Birdeye/Google, plus BBB complaints filed against REDX, and named outcomes published on REDX’s own case-study pages. No first-party customer interviews were conducted this engagement; the four interviews in scope were all REDX executives.4

Public reviews skew to the loud ends. People write when they are thrilled or furious, and the satisfied middle rarely posts. BBB complaints over-weight the angriest cases by design. The emotional registers, the buyer-and-user split, the decision timeline, and the win/loss forces in this report are all quotable and sourced, sampled from a distribution that under-represents the quiet majority.

The first recommended next step is a primary-interview pass: commission a short program of AI-led interviews with current REDX customers, recent cancellations, and agents who chose a competitor. That pass would confirm or sharpen four things the public record can only sketch: the emotional registers below, the split between what the agent who pays wants and what the person dialing wants, the decision timeline from first thought to daily habit, and the forces that win or lose a subscription. Run that interview pass before any messaging built on this report ships at scale.

1. What Customers Say, in Their Words

1.1 “If you are willing to do the work”

The strongest REDX advocates never describe the product as a lead machine that works on its own. They describe a tool that rewards effort, and they attach the condition out loud.

“What I like most about RedX is that it is a tool that you can build your business with just that one tool alone (if you are willing to do the work)”1 Persona: Working Agent. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“RedX is a great product for real estate that offers countless leads and can be the only tool you need to build a succulent business if you do the work!”5 Persona: Working Agent. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“More conversation equals more conversion. That’s what this platform offers and I love it.”6 Persona: Working Agent. Stage: Use & Succeed.

This conditional shows up in nearly every positive review, and it tells the messaging exactly who to talk to. The agent who reads “if you do the work” as a fair deal is the buyer who stays. The same condition appears on the premium competitor: a Vulcan7 advocate credits the daily-calling discipline first, accuracy second. “the dialer will really push you to make your calls each day, and the information has a higher level of accuracy than the competition that I have tried. I pay for the annual membership.”7

1.2 “Too many bad numbers”

Data accuracy is the loudest negative theme in the entire review corpus, and it appears across REDX, Vulcan7, and Landvoice alike. The complaint takes three concrete forms: dead and disconnected numbers, DNC-flagged numbers sold as callable, and “expired” leads that already sold or relisted.

“Too many ‘bad’ phone numbers. Hard to know which ones are ‘good’”2 Persona: Working Agent. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“It also contains ALOT of bad numbers as well as old numbers. I wish there was a way to get cleaner data from this so we are not wasting as much time.”8 Persona: Working Agent. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“Sometimes the data is not the most accurate since they pull from so many sources and give so many phone numbers. Less phone numbers at higher quality would be somewhat helpful”1 Persona: Working Agent. Stage: Use & Succeed.

The angriest version of the complaint shows up in BBB filings, where customers name specifics.

“I called over 50 numbers and they were either a wrong number or disconnected. I received leads with all phone numbers marked as DNC and no email address… I received leads whose property was actively listed on the market and that were already working with an agent.”9 Persona: Frustrated Switcher. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“I crossed checked sources using the MLS, Zillow and white pages but lacked consistency… I managed to get a hold of 2 people and were told that their houses sold years ago as well. The Expireds are inaccurate”10 Persona: Frustrated Switcher. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“Most of the numbers that were provided were not working, disconnected, or on the [DNC] list. I demand a full refund as I believe that they are a fraudulent company that falsely advertises.”11 Persona: Frustrated Switcher. Stage: Use & Succeed.

The same failure follows the competitors. On Vulcan7: “three months into the program and paying more than $900 for their subscription the answer is 0, that is right 0 I have been calling consistently but the phone keeps ringing and ringing and ringing very few times someone picks up and when they do is the wrong number or that person already bought the home so the information they give you is for sure not accurate.”12 On Landvoice: “Landvoice is consistently late with their data or it is entirely incorrect… I called some expired listings but the people I reached had not listed their homes in years, making me look unprofessional. This issue persists and I plan on leaving it for Vulcan 7.”13 And again on Landvoice: “Half of the property information in my own neighborhood was outdated or completely wrong. I don’t feel I can trust the data provided anymore.”14 Accuracy is a problem the whole category owns; no vendor has solved it, and the agent who leaves REDX over it often lands on a rival with the same complaint waiting.

1.3 “Save me the time”

A distinct positive theme: REDX takes the manual hunt off the agent’s plate so the hours go to selling.

“REDx is saving me the time from manualy searching for expired and FSBO information. This free my time to best serve current clients.”15 Persona: Working Agent. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“They send my Realtor great expired leads as soon as they expire on the MLS. The lists are updated regularly when a property goes active again.”16 Persona: Working Agent. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“Without this system, I’m not even sure I would pursue expired listings, for sale by owner properties, or other leads.”18 Persona: Working Agent. Stage: Use & Succeed.

Eric T.’s line is the retention argument in one sentence: the product is the reason the prospecting work happens at all.

2. The Emotional Undercurrent

The reviews carry four named emotions, each anchored in the words customers chose.

Conditional pride runs through the advocates. They are proud of results, and they credit their own effort first. “You scale your own business and can control your time. All of the auto text and email features make it easier to follow up too.”19 The pride is in the scaling and the control; the tool earns a supporting credit.

Betrayal runs through the data complaints, sharper than ordinary dissatisfaction. The agent feels sold a thing they did not receive.

“I was sold a service for callable real estate leads—not a list riddled with DNC-prohibited numbers. Providing DNC numbers while charging for said leads is deceptive and materially misrepresents the value of the product.”20 Persona: Frustrated Switcher. Stage: Use & Succeed.

The word “deceptive” is the tell. This customer feels cheated on the core promise.

Resignation under cost pressure runs through the agents who leave and come back. One filed a complaint about reliability and named the reason they returned anyway. “I have lost many days of prospecting and business opportunities because of their failure to have a proper system that is reliable… prospecting is the way I find business and I cannot go without a system such as this one. I have… been forced to come back because it’s competitor Volcan7 is very expensive.”21 The switching cost is real and the customer says it plainly: the alternative costs more, so they return even while angry.

Suspicion of the coaching runs through a smaller but vivid set. The training that REDX treats as a strength reads, to a frustrated agent, as a performance.

“Save yourself the agony and steer clear of RedX… Avoid the hours wasted watching ineffective cold-calling tutorials – they’re more interested in chatting with a facade, videos designed to showcase the success of privileged realtors while duping others.”22 Persona: Skeptical Buyer. Stage: Use & Succeed.

The same coaching layer that inspires the advocate reads as a sales facade to the cynic, who sees only curated wins from “privileged realtors.”

The buyer-and-user split. In residential prospecting the agent usually pays for the tool and also dials it, so the buyer and the user are one person early on. The split opens later, on teams. When a team leader or broker buys and an assistant or junior agent works the leads, the two roles want opposite things. The person dialing wants fewer, cleaner, callable numbers: “Less phone numbers at higher quality would be somewhat helpful.”1 The person paying wants volume and coverage to justify the subscription and feed the whole team. A review filed by an administrative assistant on behalf of “my Realtor” shows the buyer and the dialer are already separate people in part of the base.16 Message the value to both: coverage and ROI to the buyer who signs, callable-number quality and time-saved to the agent who dials. A primary-interview pass should size this split, because it drives whether the messaging segments by role.

3. Why They Hired

The decision path from first thought to daily habit shows up across the reviews, and it is short.

The push that starts it is the grind of manual prospecting. Agents describe lead-hunting as a time sink that the product removes. “I love how I can use the system without having to spend time on research… I don’t like that there is never enough time to get through all the leads.”23 The push is wanting the research hours back.

The pull that closes it is the promise of more seller conversations on the agent’s own schedule. “More conversation equals more conversion. That’s what this platform offers and I love it.”6 The buyer is sold on conversation volume they control.

First use comes fast. REDX’s own published customer describes a low starting threshold and an immediate result. “It is 99.99% impossible to log into REDX and have 20 real estate conversations every day and not get a listing or an appointment within that first 30-day contract period.”24 Whatever the figure, the customer is describing a product that produces a result inside the first contract period, which is what turns a trial into a habit.

  • Habit is the goal, and customers name it. “REDX keeps me disciplined in lead management, providing and organizing leads effectively for follow-up.”3 The Vulcan7 advocate says the same about a rival: the daily dialer push is the feature.7 Agents are buying a system that makes them prospect, and the ones who internalize the daily habit are the ones who stay.

4. Why They Almost Didn’t

The hesitations and objections, in the four forces that work against a purchase.

  • Anxiety about data quality is the dominant objection, and it is rational given the review corpus. A prospect reading reviews mid-decision sees the bad-numbers complaint everywhere. The unvarnished version: “I would not use REDX. They give out numbers that don’t work or are outdated. Many address don’t have phone numbers. And the email subscription is just not worth it. Not many property owners have their email address on their list.”25

Anxiety about reliability compounds it. When onboarding breaks, the agent cannot run their business, and they say so in operational terms. “RedX data and customer service are trash. After 5 business days, 5 phone calls and wasting 6 working hours, RedX is unable to get my account up and running… There is no way to run a business working with them and tools that are bound to break/not work as advertised.”26

The habit force cuts both ways. The same switching cost that returns lapsed customers also keeps unhappy ones tethered. The agent who came back because Vulcan7 “is very expensive” did not return out of loyalty; they returned because leaving cost more than staying.21

A pull objection sits inside the product itself: agents want better qualification before they spend dial time.

“Although you get several leads per month, I’d still like to have better qualification questions to ask BEFORE I get the lead.”27 Persona: Working Agent. Stage: Evaluation.

The agent has plenty of volume and wants to spend limited hours on the leads most likely to transact. That points to where the product can reduce the buyer’s risk before purchase.

5. The Language Gap

Customers and vendors describe the same product with different words. The vendor column below holds the category cliche every lead vendor uses, drawn at the category level. The shift column is the language to adopt.

What customers say What vendors say The shift to make
“bad numbers,” “wrong number or disconnected,” “on the DNC”2 “verified,” “accurate,” “most accurate data” Stop claiming purity. Talk about confidence-rated numbers and honest hit-rate expectations set at onboarding.
“already sold,” “already working with an agent,” “houses sold years ago”9 “motivated sellers,” “must-transact owners” Drop the universal cliche. Lead on documented conversion-when-worked and named-agent outcomes.
“sold callable leads” but got “a list riddled with DNC-prohibited numbers”20 “leads” Distinguish a record from a callable contact. Name what is callable, compliant, and reachable.
“videos designed to showcase the success of privileged realtors”22 “coaching,” “training,” “community” Show realistic, named-peer results and the work behind them, not curated highlight reels.
“if you do the work,” “more conversation equals more conversion”1 “lead generation,” “done-for-you leads” Sell the conditional honestly: the complete system plus coaching that pays off for the working agent.

Words to adopt: “if you do the work,” “more conversation equals more conversion,” “keeps me disciplined,” “the only tool you need,” “saves me the time,” callable, confidence-rated, named-agent results.

Words to drop: “most accurate data,” “verified data,” “motivated sellers” as a standalone headline, “done-for-you,” “passive,” any purity or superiority claim about the numbers.

6. What Customers Feel but Do Not Say

Three patterns the quotes support without stating outright.

  • They expect to do the work, and they resent being sold otherwise. The advocates volunteer the condition (“if you are willing to do the work”1). The harshest critics are the agents who expected passive results and felt deceived.22 Customers arrive expecting effort, so marketing that promises effortless leads manufactures its own worst reviews from the agents it disappoints.
  • They blame the data, but the deeper fear is wasted time. Every bad-number complaint is really a complaint about dial hours burned on dead records: “so we are not wasting as much time,”8 “Less phone numbers at higher quality.”1 The unvoiced ask is for fewer, higher-confidence numbers, so the scarce resource, the agent’s calling time, goes to live owners. No vendor delivers perfect data, and these customers are not asking for it.
  • They know they are stuck, and they resent it. The customers who leave and return do the math on switching cost and come back resentful even as they re-subscribe.21 The unspoken risk for REDX: a tethered, unhappy customer is one good competitor offer away from gone. This retention rests partly on Vulcan7’s price, a lever REDX does not control.

7. Rave Notes: A Testimonial Bank

Real, cited advocacy verbatims a copywriter can pull from directly, grouped by what they prove.

On building a business with one tool

“What I like most about RedX is that it is a tool that you can build your business with just that one tool alone (if you are willing to do the work)”1 Persona: Working Agent. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“RedX is a great product for real estate that offers countless leads and can be the only tool you need to build a succulent business if you do the work!”5 Persona: Working Agent. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“Without this system, I’m not even sure I would pursue expired listings, for sale by owner properties, or other leads.”18 Persona: Working Agent. Stage: Use & Succeed.

On conversations and conversion

“More conversation equals more conversion. That’s what this platform offers and I love it.”6 Persona: Working Agent. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“It is 99.99% impossible to log into REDX and have 20 real estate conversations every day and not get a listing or an appointment within that first 30-day contract period.”24 Persona: Working Agent. Stage: Use & Succeed.

On discipline and habit

“REDX keeps me disciplined in lead management, providing and organizing leads effectively for follow-up.”3 Persona: Disciplined Prospector. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“You scale your own business and can control your time. All of the auto text and email features make it easier to follow up too”19 Persona: Working Agent. Stage: Use & Succeed.

On saving time

“REDx is saving me the time from manualy searching for expired and FSBO information. This free my time to best serve current clients.”15 Persona: Working Agent. Stage: Use & Succeed.
“They send my Realtor great expired leads as soon as they expire on the MLS. The lists are updated regularly when a property goes active again.”16 Persona: Working Agent. Stage: Use & Succeed.

Every quote above is a real, sourced review. Vortex, REDX’s free lead manager, also has a published productivity testimonial on REDX’s case-study wall worth confirming for direct use.28

8. Strategic Implications

Each implication translates what customers say into a move that closes the gap between the market’s perception and REDX’s source of truth.

Commission a primary-interview and case-study program before scaling any messaging. This report reads from public reviews and BBB complaints only; no current customer was interviewed this engagement.4 Run a short program of AI-led interviews with current customers, recent cancellations, and agents who chose a rival, and confirm the four registers named in the Evidence Base section. Sequence this first, before the messaging guide’s claims ship at scale.

Lead the brand on the conditional promise the advocates already voice, and demote the data claim. Customers describe the win as a complete system plus coaching that pays off “if you do the work.”1 Build the headline identity on that, name the work-willing agent as the buyer, and retire any data-purity or “most accurate” claim from the headline. This reinforces the Brief’s lead recommendation and the messaging guide’s primary pillar.

Fix the data-hygiene experience first, then claim parity-at-lower-cost, never superiority. Bad, disconnected, and DNC-flagged numbers are the loudest complaint across REDX and every rival.9 De-dupe DNC and disconnected records, tighten the confidence-rating filter toward fewer, higher-quality numbers as customers ask (“Less phone numbers at higher quality would be somewhat helpful”)1, and set honest hit-rate expectations at onboarding. Hold a hard cap on any accuracy over-claim until the hygiene work ships.

Prove lead quality with named outcomes, not the “motivated seller” cliche. Customers read “motivated sellers” as the table-stakes line every vendor uses, and they puncture it with “already sold” and “houses sold years ago.”10 Anchor the lead-quality story on documented, named-agent conversion outcomes from the case-study library and retire the unaudited percentages until they are confirmed.

Reframe the coaching around named-peer proof and the work behind it. The training reads as a “facade” to skeptical agents who see only curated success.22 Show the dial counts and the months behind each named-agent win, so the coaching layer converts the work-willing buyer instead of triggering the cynic.

Manage the review surface as a sales channel. Prospects read public reviews and BBB complaints mid-decision, so the review surface is now part of the sales floor. The accuracy complaints are the first thing a careful buyer finds.11 Respond to BBB complaints substantively, surface the named-agent case studies where prospects look, and route satisfied advocates to G2 and Google to rebalance a corpus that currently over-weights the angriest voices. Assign an owner to the review surface this quarter.

Sources

  • G2, Marios M. (Owner, Small-Business), 2026-02-03, https://www.g2.com/products/redx/reviews

  • BBB complaint #24316226, REDX customer (multi-year), 2025-12-24, https://www.bbb.org/us/ut/orem/profile/sales-lead-generation/redx-llc-1166-22014712/complaints
